original_question:
A hemisphere with radius $200$ sits on top of a horizontal circular disk with radius $200,$ and the hemisphere and disk have the same center. Let $\mathcal T$ be the region of points P in the disk such that a sphere of radius $42$ can be placed on top of the disk at $P$ and lie completely inside the hemisphere. The area of $\mathcal T$ divided by the area of the disk is $\tfrac pq,$ where $p$ and $q$ are relatively prime positive integers. Find $p+q.$

Present the answer in LaTex format: \boxed{Your answer}

Let \mathcal T be the region of points P in the disk such that a sphere of radius 30 can be placed on top of the disk at P and lie completely inside the hemisphere. The area of \mathcal T divided by the area of the disk is \tfrac pq, where p and q are relatively prime positive integers. Find p+q.

variants.variant: A hemisphere with a radius of [BLANK] rests on a flat circular disk that has a radius of [BLANK], with both the hemisphere and disk sharing the same center. Define \mathcal T as the set of points P within the disk where a sphere of radius 30 can be positioned on the disk at P and remain entirely within the hemisphere. The ratio of the area of \mathcal T to the area of the disk is expressed as \tfrac pq, where p and q are coprime positive integers. Determine the sum p+q.
